headline: Stillwater County Created, 1913
body:
The year of our Lord 1913, a time when the great state of Montana, still young and rambunctious, found itself in a peculiar predicament: growing pains. Like a strapping lad outgrowing his britches, the existing counties, Carbon, Sweet Grass, and Yellowstone, were simply too vast, their administrative reach stretched thin across the sprawling plains and formidable mountains. The populace, ever increasing with the promise of rich soil and richer ore, clamored for closer governance, for a voice that didn't echo across a hundred miles of wilderness before being heard. It was a matter of practicality, you see, a common sense notion that even a mule could grasp, given enough oats and a clear path.

And so, the wheels of progress, or perhaps, the wheels of bureaucracy, began to turn. The good citizens, with a keen eye on their pocketbooks, saw the wisdom in a new arrangement. A local paper, the Absarokee Enterprise, with a headline as direct as a punch to the nose, declared, "Stillwater County is a Necessity." They laid bare the financial burdens of the elder counties: Carbon, burdened with a debt of $290,575; Yellowstone, carrying $201,090; and Sweet Grass, a comparatively modest $133,087. The proposed new county, they argued, would shoulder a mere $13,249 after adjustments, a sum that promised a lighter tax levy and, by extension, happier taxpayers. It was a compelling argument, one that resonated with the pioneering spirit of self-reliance and fiscal prudence.

The vote, held on March 15, 1913, was a resounding affirmation. Eight hundred and thirty-two souls cast their lot in favor, with only one hundred and forty-six clinging to the old ways. Thus, Stillwater County, the thirty-fourth jewel in Montana’s crown, was born. And what a birth it was! A mere sixteen days later, on March 31, the town of Columbus, chosen as the county seat, erupted in a celebration that would make a lesser town blush. Despite roads that were, by all accounts, little more than muddy wagon trails, a testament to the untamed nature of the land, the people came.

The halls of the Columbus State Bank swelled with four hundred citizens, their faces alight with the promise of a new era. Senator Patrick Patten and Representative John Corwin, men of considerable oratorical prowess, held forth, their words painting a vivid picture of the county’s bright future. But the true spirit of the frontier, that irrepressible joy in the face of hardship, truly shone when the speeches concluded. The Columbus Opera House, a bastion of culture in the wild West, became the stage for a grand dance. The Park City band, the Columbus Ideal orchestra, and the Absarokee orchestra, a veritable symphony of local talent, played on, their melodies weaving through the night. Over eight hundred revelers, their boots thumping and spirits soaring, danced until the clock struck three in the morning, a testament to the enduring optimism and communal joy that marked the founding of Stillwater County.
